content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF page 202 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TH_003_page_001.jpg

---

**1** When we could not bear it any longer, we decided it was best to be left behind in Athens alone,

**2** and we sent Timothy — our brother and God's coworker in the good news of Christ — to strengthen you and encourage you in your faith,

**3** so that none of you would be shaken by these troubles. You know very well that this is what we are destined for.

**4** Even when we were with you, we kept warning you: "We are going to face suffering" — and that is exactly what happened, as you well know.

**5** That is why, when I could stand it no longer, I sent to find out about your faith — afraid that the tempter had somehow gotten to you and that all our hard work had been for nothing.

**6** But now Timothy has just come back to us from you, bringing us the wonderful news of your faith and love. He tells us that you always hold us in warm remembrance and that you long to see us, just as we long to see you.

**7** And so, brothers and sisters, in the middle of all our own distress and hardship, your faith has been a deep encouragement to us.

**8** Because now we truly live, knowing that you are standing firm in the Lord.

**9** How can we possibly thank God enough for you, for all the joy you bring us in the presence of our God?

**10** Night and day we pray with an intensity beyond words that we might see you face to face and supply whatever is still lacking in your faith.

**11** May God our Father himself and our Lord Jesus clear the way for us to come to you.

**12** And may the Lord cause your love to increase and overflow for one another and for everyone else — just as ours does for you —

**13** so that he may establish your hearts as blameless and holy before our God and Father, when our Lord Jesus comes with all his holy ones.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "could not bear it" (ΜΗΚΕΤΙ ΣΤΕΓΟΝΤΕΣ):** The verb ΣΤΕΓΩ means to cover, contain, or endure. Paul is saying the emotional pressure of separation became unbearable — the lid came off. "Could not bear it any longer" captures this breaking point. The decision to stay alone in Athens was a sacrifice: Paul chose isolation so Timothy could go to them.

**Verse 2 — "God's coworker" (ΣΥΝΕΡΓΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ):** The Sinaiticus reads ΣΥΝΕΡΓΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ — "coworker of God." This is a striking phrase: Timothy is presented as God's own colleague in the gospel enterprise. Some later manuscripts soften this to "servant of God" or "minister of God," likely because scribes found the original too bold. I followed the Sinaiticus reading. "Coworker" preserves the startling directness of Paul's claim.

**Verse 3 — "shaken" (ΣΑΙΝΕΣΘΑΙ):** This rare word originally described the wagging of a dog's tail, then came to mean "disturbed" or "unsettled." Paul fears the Thessalonians might be destabilized — not that they would fully abandon faith, but that suffering might loosen their footing. "Shaken" captures this sense of being knocked off balance.

**Verse 3 — "destined for" (ΕΙΣ ΤΟΥΤΟ ΚΕΙΜΕΘΑ):** Literally "we are appointed to this." Paul does not treat suffering as an accident or punishment — it is the landscape through which the faithful walk. I chose "destined for" to convey that this is not random misfortune but an expected part of the calling.

**Verse 5 — "the tempter" (Ο ΠΕΙΡΑΖΩΝ):** Paul uses a participial title — "the one who tests/tempts." Rather than inserting "Satan" (which Paul does not use here), I kept the functional title. The fear is specific: not that they have sinned, but that external pressure has eroded their trust.

**Verse 6 — "wonderful news" (ΕΥΑΓΓΕΛΙΣΑΜΕΝΟΥ):** Paul uses the verb ΕΥΑΓΓΕΛΙΖΩ — the same word used for proclaiming the gospel — to describe Timothy's report about their faith. This is deliberate and remarkable: Timothy's news about the Thessalonians' steadfastness is itself a kind of gospel announcement. "Wonderful news" preserves the weight of this word choice without overexplaining it.

**Verse 7 — "distress and hardship" (ΑΝΑΓΚΗ ΚΑΙ ΘΛΙΨΙΣ):** Two words for suffering stacked together. ΑΝΑΓΚΗ is necessity, compulsion, dire constraint. ΘΛΙΨΙΣ is pressure, crushing affliction. Paul's own situation is bleak — but their faith breaks through his darkness like light.

**Verse 8 — "now we truly live" (ΝΥΝ ΖΩΜΕΝ):** This is one of the most emotionally raw lines in Paul's letters. He equates their faithfulness with his own vitality. Without confirmation of their standing, he was suffocating. Their steadfastness is his oxygen.

**Verse 10 — "intensity beyond words" (ΥΠΕΡΕΚΠΕΡΙΣΣΟΥ):** This compound adverb piles prefix upon prefix — ΥΠΕΡ (beyond) + ΕΚ (out of) + ΠΕΡΙΣΣΟΥ (abundance). Paul is reaching for language and finding it insufficient. The prayer is not casual; it is desperate, excessive, overflowing. "Intensity beyond words" tries to honor this linguistic straining.

**Verse 11 — "clear the way" (ΚΑΤΕΥΘΥΝΑΙ):** Literally "make straight the road." The optative mood signals a prayer-wish. Paul asks God to remove the obstacles that have prevented his return. I used "clear the way" to preserve the sense of path-making — someone removing barriers so the journey becomes possible.

**Verse 13 — "holy ones" (ΑΓΙΩΝ):** The Sinaiticus reads ΑΓΙΩΝ ΑΥΤΟΥ — "his holy ones." This likely refers to angels, though some interpreters include departed believers. I kept "holy ones" without specifying, as Paul himself leaves the category open.

**Codex Sinaiticus Observations:** Chapter 3 begins in the latter portion of column 2, with verse 2 visible near the top of that column and the text running continuously through column 3, where verse 10 begins. The manuscript clearly reads ΣΥΝΕΡΓΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ in verse 2, a reading that later traditions frequently altered. The text flows without paragraph breaks in scriptio continua. The transition from chapter 3 to chapter 4 occurs in column 3, with 4:1 beginning partway through. The manuscript shows no significant corrections or marginal notes in this section.

— PNEUMA
